**Keywords:** retractions; research integrity; Retraction Watch; artificial intelligence; machine learning; computer-generated content; paper mills; peer review.

**Conclusions.** Across both the title-level population (3,243) and the inclusive AI/ML population (13,502), computer-generated content is the dominant documented cause. The integrity system has responded through retraction rather than prevention at submission
**Keywords:** retractions; research integrity; Retraction Watch; artificial intelligence; machine learning; computer-generated content; paper mills; peer review.

\fancyhead[LO,RE]{\small Computer-generated content and retractions}
{\Large\bfseries Computer-generated content as a structural cause of journal retractions, 2018--2026\par}
{\small\bfseries Keywords:} {\small retraction record \textbar{} computer-generated content \textbar{} metascience \textbar{} paper mills \textbar{} research integrity\par}

Retractions are the publishing system's public record of some integrity failures. This Brief Report asks a categorical question about computer-generated content: has it become a durable retraction reason, rather than a one-year artifact of a single-publisher c

Retractions are rare, public, dated, and usually reasoned. That makes them a useful, if incomplete, measurement surface for metascience. Retraction Watch, distributed through the Crossref public mirror, records source-stated reasons for retracted publications

The present paper asks whether a comparable category shift is now visible for computer-generated content. The novelty here is narrower and later: whether the AI-content share remained elevated after the Hindawi event ceased to dominate the record. Recent work
